coredata.dc:title: Lanthanum-doped carbon dots for anticancer drug transport
coredata.dc:creator: Joy Williams, Monisha
Raboni Grace, Sudhaker
Manikantan, Varnitha
Alexander, Aleyamma
Sri Varalakshmi, Govindaraj
Ramasamy, Sivaraj
Sumohan Pillai, Archana
V.M.V. Enoch, Israel
Núñez, Dariela
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we report lanthanum-doped carbon dots for delivering the anticancer drug camptothecin. The carbon dots are made as a nanocomposite with a β-cyclodextrin-polyethylene glycol-folate polymer. XRD shows a typical reflection of carbon dots (002). The size of the carbon dot composite is 10.19 nm. XPS shows peaks at 830 and 850 eV, characteristic of lanthanum doped in the carbon dots. The material is superparamagnetic with a saturation magnetization of 0.28 emu/g. The nanocomposite shows a broad absorption band with tail absorption in the near-infrared range. Further, the material shows a high camptothecin encapsulation efficiency above 90 % and a slow release for above 5 days. The in vitro anticancer effect of the drug-loaded nanocarrier is examined on MCF-7 cell lines, and the IC50 value is 1.92 µg mL−1. The cell cycle arrest is analyzed using flow cytometry. The nanocarrier shows promising results as a drug delivery agent.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanoparticles
Carbon materials
Nanocomposites
Polymer
β-Cyclodextrin
Drug delivery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CD-PEG-fol polymer coated C-dots:La
Synthesis and analysis of C-dots:La structure
Magnetic and optical properties of NC
Drug loading on the NC and release of drug

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A. XRD, B. TEM, C. particle size distribution of polymer-containing C-dots:La.

A. X-ray Photoelectron survey spectrum of polymer-containing C-dots:La; Deconvoluted XP spectra of B. C 1 s, C. O 1 s, D. N 1 s, and E. La; F. Magnetization curves of as-synthesized and polymer-containing C-dots:La; G. UV–vis-NIR absorption spectrum.

A. Drug release from the NC; MCF-7-cell viabilities on treatment with B. NC and C. CPT@NC; Cells treated with D–J. NC and K –Q. CPT@NC; EtBr-AO assay: R. Control cells, S. CPT@NC-treated cells. T. Percentage of cells in various stages on treatment with CPT@NC.
Methods of syntheses of CD-PEG-fol and C-dot:La@CD-PEG-fol NC.

head.ce:title: Lanthanum-doped carbon dots for anticancer drug transport

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Departamento de Química Ambiental, Facultad de Ciencias, Universidad Católica de la Santísima Concepción, Concepción, Chile

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Universidad Católica de la Santísima Concepción

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Departamento de Química Ambiental, Facultad de Ciencias, Universidad Católica de la Santísima Concepción, Concepción, Chile

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we report lanthanum-doped carbon dots for delivering the anticancer drug camptothecin. The carbon dots are made as a nanocomposite with a β-cyclodextrin-polyethylene glycol-folate polymer. XRD shows a typical reflection of carbon dots (002). The size of the carbon dot composite is 10.19 nm. XPS shows peaks at 830 and 850 eV, characteristic of lanthanum doped in the carbon dots. The material is superparamagnetic with a saturation magnetization of 0.28 emu/g. The nanocomposite shows a broad absorption band with tail absorption in the near-infrared range. Further, the material shows a high camptothecin encapsulation efficiency above 90 % and a slow release for above 5 days. The in vitro anticancer effect of the drug-loaded nanocarrier is examined on MCF-7 cell lines, and the IC50 value is 1.92 µg mL−1. The cell cycle arrest is analyzed using flow cytometry. The nanocarrier shows promising results as a drug delivery agent.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Various nanocarriers have been explored, and each has its advantages and demerits as anticancer drug vehicles [1]. As cancer is hard-to-cure and acquires drug resistance over time, it is essential to engineer nanocarriers with newer functionalities and compositions to improve efficiency [2]. Curing breast cancer is difficult at later stages of its development [3]. Due to the poor target availability of chemotherapeutics, their efficacy needs improvement [4]. Carbon dots (C-dots) represent a newer class of carbon nanomaterials [5]. Doped C-dots possess intriguing properties and have been employed in directed drug delivery [6]. Lanthanum imparts unique magnetic and spectroscopic features to materials and coordination compounds formed by them [7]. Therefore, it is intriguing to dope C-dots with La and study their properties.

Nanocomposites have been employed to integrate essential properties and fine-tune applications in drug delivery [8–10]. C-dots with longer wavelength absorption, high drug-carrying capacity, and appreciable targeting potential require a delicate design and have not been reported with a focus on combining these properties. We hypothesized it could be achieved by nanocomposites of lanthanide-doped C-dots with β-Cyclodextrin (β-CD). β-CD is a cyclic glucose heptamer possessing a hydrophobic inner rim and a hydrophilic exterior [11]. β-CD allows sustained release of the encapsulated drug [12]. β-CD linked with folate through a spacer polyethylene glycol (PEG) group enables folate receptor-mediated endocytosis [13]. Therefore, we synthesized a nanocomposite (NC) of a β-CD-PEG-folate with La-doped C-dots as an anticancer drug carrier, with the following characteristics of the coating polymer: (i) it carries a β-CD for encapsulating CPT (ii) a PEG spacer suitable to escape macrophage uptake, and (iii) a folate ligand which allows endocytosis through folate receptors overexpressed in cancer cells.

Lanthanum nitrate, Ethylene diamine tetra acetic acid disodium salt (EDTA), PEG (M.W.: 400), β-CD, sodium azide, propargyl bromide, methanesulfonyl chloride, and camptothecin (>98 %) were procured from Merck. MCF-7 cell lines were purchased from NCC Pune; DMEM Glutamax, MTT, and DMSO from ThermoFischer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CD-PEG-fol polymer coated C-dots:La
ce:section.ce:para: Lanthanum nitrate (0.01 M) and EDTA (0.01 M) and 0.1 g (0.05 mM) of the synthesized polymer were completely dissolved in 15 mL of DI water and autoclaved for 6 h at 180 °C. The precipitate was removed using filtration and then dialyzed using cellulose ester dialysis tubing (MWCO: 5000 kDa). The desired nanoparticle is obtained by lyophilizing the dialyzed solution.
For information on polymer synthesis, drug release, cytotoxicity, and instrumentation, see Supplementary Information, SI 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and analysis of C-dots:La structure
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM image (Fig. 1B) shows the C-dots:La as spherical particles with a mean size of 10.19 nm. Further evidence for the size range was added using DLS. Fig. 1C shows the particle size distribution with the mean hydrodynamic diameter as 17.56 nm.

The composition of the NC was analyzed using XPS. Fig. 2 A, B, C, and D depict the C 1 s, O 1 s, N 1 s, and La 3d spectra respectively. The XPS of C 1 s is deconvoluted into the peaks, at binding energies 284.3, 285.7, 287.4, and 289.6 eV, ascribed to the C 1 s states of C = C, C–N, C–C, and C = O carbons, respectively. The XPS of O 1 s (Fig. 2B) comprises peaks at BE’s of 529.8 and 531.9 eV, corresponding to the C = O and C–O oxygens. Fig. 2C shows a peak deconvoluted to C–N and N–C = O, arising from N 1 s states (399.2 and 400.3 eV respectively). La shows peaks at 834.9, 838.0, and 851.7 eV, of the La 3d5/2, La 3d5/2 spin–orbit split state, and La 3d3/2 states, respectively (Fig. 2D) [14]. Thus, XPS reveals the presence of La3+ in the C-dots as a dopant and the polymer as a composite (For discussion on FT-IR and TGA, see SI 2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Magnetic and optical properties of NC
ce:section.ce:para: The magnetization of the as-prepared C-dots:La and the NC (Fig. 2E) show sigmoidal curves with negligible coercivity. The saturation magnetization (MS) of the as-prepared C-dots:La is 0.28 emu g−1, whereas, for NC, it is 0.19 emu g−1, indicating super-paramagnetism in C-dots.

The UV–vis-NIR (Fig. 2F) of the NC (1 mg mL−1) shows a broad band in the 500–800 nm range. In the NIR region, the absorbance value is above 0.05, enabling laser excitation in this wavelength range. The properties suggest that C-dots:La NC are suitable for imaging applications [15].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug loading on the NC and release of drug
ce:section.ce:para: The drug loading efficiency and the release kinetics are examined using the drug camptothecin (CPT). The calculated EE is 89.37 %. The in vitro release of CPT as a function at two pH conditions (7.4 and 6.0) was followed. Fig. 3 A presents the slow and sustained cumulative drug release against release time. Up to about 130 h (5½ days), the release percentage was about 30 % at pH 7.4. The release is carried out at two different pH to investigate the effect of incremental acidic condition as a stimulus for drug expulsion. Change of pH leads to difference in binding strengths of drugs with cyclodextrin, the acidic condition usually lowering the binding constant value [16]. The release at the slightly acidic pH (6.0) is more rapid, and at 5½ days, the release has reached 50 %. At 24 and 48 h, the cumulative releases are 18.54 % and 22.89 % at pH 7.4 and 13.42 % and 23.48 % at pH 6.0. Such a sustained release allows drug concentration in the circulatory.

The in vitro cytotoxicity of the free NC and CPT-loaded NC (CPT@NC) was determined on MCF-7 cells. The cell viabilities are plotted against the concentration of samples (NC and CPT@NC) in Fig. 3B and 3C.

The histograms indicate dose-dependent toxicity of NC and CPT@NC (0.1–100 mg mL−1 range). The cytotoxicity of the empty NC is low, and the IC50 value is 39.3 % mg mL−1. Contrarily, the cytotoxicity of the CPT@NC is high (IC50 = 1.92 mg mL−1) when measured after 24 h of incubation. Therefore, the inhibitory activity of the CPT@NC is due to the partial release of CPT from the NC. Such cytotoxicity with a partial release of drug has been reported in doped carbon dots [17]. Slow release from the NC results in the availability of the drug to the cell lines in mild surges rather than rapid increase of concentration as in the case of free CPT. This continuous availability of drug enables the cellular uptake and enhances the cell inhibitory activity [18]. The IC50 of free CPT without loading on any carrier has been reported as 12.51 mg mL−1 [19].

An Ethidium bromide/Acridine Orange (EtBr/AO) assay of cell death due to the CPT@NC is carried out. The control and the CPT@NC-treated cells are shown in Fig. 3R and S, respectively. When the MCF-7 cells were treated with the NC, about 73 % were in the early apoptotic phase, and about 16 % in the pro-apoptotic and apoptotic phases (Fig. 3T). In the CPT@NC treatment groups, 69 % of cells were in early apoptosis, and 13 % in pro-apoptosis and late apoptosis. These show that the NC alone and drug-loaded NC induced combined necrosis and apoptosis. Therefore, the CPT loaded on the NC shows an enhanced efficacy and the polymer-containing C-Dot:La is promising as a suitable nanocarrier for the chemotherapeutic drug.

Lanthanum-doped C-dots, made as a composite with CD-PEG400-fol, are synthesized. The La3+ is bonded to the surface functional groups of the C-dots. The material is superparamagnetic, owing to the presence of La, introducing its desired magnetic property. The long tail absorption extends up to 800 nm and above, indicating its suitability for NIR-laser excitation. The drug loading efficiency is high, a rational property of polymers with tethered β-CD. The release of CPT from the nanocarrier is prolonged and depends on the pH condition. It reveals that the drug release can be accelerated at lower pH, typical of tumor microenvironments. The toxicity of the empty nanocarrier is low. The drug-loaded nanocarrier shows breast cancer cell inhibition, dose-dependently, and the IC50 value is 1.92 mg mL−1.

Monisha Joy Williams: Data curation. Sudhaker Raboni Grace: Data curation. Varnitha Manikantan: Data curation. Aleyamma Alexander: Formal analysis, Validation. Govindaraj Sri Varalakshmi: Data curation. Sivaraj Ramasamy: Supervision. Archana Sumohan Pillai: Supervision. Israel V.M.V. Enoch: Supervision. Dariela Núñez: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2023.135540.

ce:section.ce:para:
